text: The surgeon begins with a lateral to medial dissection along the white line of Toldt, medializing the left colon and progressing towards the left side of the rectal mesentery. The ureter is identified during this process to ensure safe dissection before proceeding to the medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon flips the sigmoid colon over, elevating the rectal mesentery with assistance to keep it out of the pelvis. The medial to lateral dissection begins, progressing towards the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, dissecting it proximal to the takeoff of the superior rectal and left colic arteries. The IMA pedicle appears short, and the surgeon continues to work higher to isolate the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon releases tension on the vessel before proceeding with the medial to lateral dissection, separating the left colic mesentery from the retroperitoneum. The dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, providing additional length on the descending colon for the anastomosis.

The surgeon encounters atypical adhesions of the colonica papilloica and omentum at the splenic flexure, carefully dissecting these adhesions to ensure sufficient length. The lateral mobilization is completed up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon selects the proximal resection margin, approximately ten centimeters above the tumor, and clears the mesentery using a vessel sealer. A specimen extraction is performed through a fan and steel incision, slightly larger than five centimeters due to the tumor size. The surgeon uses a purse-string device to place the anvil back in. The rectum is cleared at the distal resection margin, and ICG is utilized to assess perfusion at both the distal and proximal resection margins, confirming good perfusion.

The surgeon uses a blue load on the stapler to transect the rectum, checking the reach before undocking the robot. The anvil is placed, and a 29mm EEA stapler is used to complete the anastomosis from below, with assistance holding the anvil at the bed.
